1: bayyôm hahûʾ niqqrāʾ bəsēfer Mōšeh bəʾoznê hāʿām — 'On that day they read from the Book of Moses in the hearing of the people.' The passive niphal niqqrāʾ (H7121) = 'was read' — public, communal reading of the written text triggers the reform. The pattern: Torah text read aloud → community discovers what is written → immediate application. This mirrors the Josiah reform (2 Kings 22:8-13), the Ezra assembly (Nehemiah 8:1), and the Josiah-era Deuteronomy discovery. Written Scripture drives covenant renewal throughout Ezra-Nehemiah.
2: ʾăšer lōʾ-qiḏdəmû ʾeṯ-bənê yiśrāʾēl ballāḥem ûḇammāyim — 'who had not welcomed the sons of Israel with bread and water.' The Deuteronomic disqualification of Ammonites and Moabites (Deuteronomy 23:3-5) is grounded in historical covenant failure, not race: they refused hospitality and hired Balaam to curse. The verb qiḏdem (H6923, piel = to go before, meet proactively with welcome) denotes active hospitality that was absent. wayyahaḵēp̄ ʾĕlōhênû ʾeṯ-haqqəlālāh liḇrāḵāh — 'our God turned the curse into a blessing' — hāpak (H2015, qal = to turn, overturn) describes YHWH's sovereign reversal of Balaam's hired curse (Num 22-24). The citation from Moses grounds the community's action in covenant precedent, not Nehemiah's personal authority.
3: wayhî kəšomʿām ʾeṯ-hattôrāh wayyabdîlû kol-ʿēreḇ miyyiśrāʾēl — 'When they heard the law, they separated from Israel all those of foreign descent (ʿēreḇ).' Wayyabdîlû (H914, hiphil = to separate, make distinct) is the same root used in Genesis 1:4 (God separated light from darkness), Leviticus 20:24 (God separated Israel from the nations), and Nehemiah 9:2; Nehemiah 10:28 (the community separated themselves). The ʿēreḇ ('mixed multitude,' H6154) echoes Exodus 12:38 where a 'mixed multitude' joined the exodus — the post-exilic community must carefully define its boundaries after learning them from Torah.
4: ûlipnê mizzeh — 'Now before this' — a narrative flashback to events that preceded the Torah reading of v1. Eliashib the priest is described as qārôḇ (H7138 = near, related) to Tobiah — the connection implies kinship or close alliance. Haqqārôḇ ləṭôḇiyyāh — 'who was allied to Tobiah.' Eliashib was the high priest (Nehemiah 3:1); that the high priestly line had formed an alliance with Nehemiah's persistent enemy (Tobiah the Ammonite, Nehemiah 2:10; Nehemiah 4:3,7; Nehemiah 6:17-19) is a severe institutional corruption — the gatekeeper of the sacred has opened the door to its opponent.
5: wayyaʿaś lô lišḵāh gəḏôlāh — 'and had set aside a large room (lišḵāh, H3957 = chamber/storeroom) for him.' The list of what the room previously held — hamminhāh, halləḇônāh, wəhaḵēlîm (grain offering, incense, vessels), the tithes of grain, wine, and oil designated for Levites, singers, and gatekeepers — constitutes the operational supply-room of the temple. The room belonged to YHWH's service; it was converted to a foreign dignitary's private quarters. The spatial desecration maps onto the theological: what was consecrated to God has been handed to the adversary.
6: ûḇəḵol-zeh lōʾ-hāyîṯî bîrûšālaim — 'While all this was happening, I was not in Jerusalem.' Nehemiah's absence explains how the corruption occurred: institutional safeguards require present, vigilant leadership. ûḇišnaṯ šəlōšîm ûšəṯayim — 'in the thirty-second year of Artaxerxes' = ca. 433 BCE, about 11 years after his arrival (444 BCE). waʾeštāl mēhammeleḵ — 'I asked of the king' (qal of šāʾal, H7592 = to ask/request). The verb marks the formal request for leave; the same root as Solomon's 'asking' for wisdom (1 Kings 3:11).
7: wāʾāḇōʾ lîrûšālaim — 'and came back to Jerusalem.' The narrative of return (second arrival, recapitulating the first of Nehemiah 2) leads immediately to confrontation: wayyāḇîn laārāʿāh ʾăšer ʿāśāh ʾĔlyāšîḇ — 'I understood the evil (rāʿāh) that Eliashib had done.' The noun rāʿāh (H7451 = evil, wickedness) is not a casual term — it places Eliashib's action in the moral category of covenant violation. Wayyāḇîn (hiphil of bîn, H995 = to cause to understand) describes Nehemiah's arrival at full comprehension of the situation: the room prepared for Tobiah in the temple courts.
8: wayyēraʿ lî məʾōḏ — 'it was very evil to me' (qal imperfect of rāʿaʿ, H7489 + preposition lə = 'was evil to/for me'). This is the same idiom as Nehemiah's initial grief in Nehemiah 2:3 (wayyēraʿ lî məʾōḏ). The emotional response parallels the first crisis: institutional evil produces visceral personal grief. wāʾašlîḵāh ʾeṯ-kol-kəlê bêṯ-Ṭôḇiyyāh haḥûṣāh — 'I threw all of Tobiah's household belongings outside.' Šālaḵ (H7993, hiphil = to throw, cast out) is forceful physical action: Nehemiah physically ejects the contents before any consultation. The immediacy models covenant zeal that does not delay for political calculation.
9: wāʾōmar wəyṭahărû haləšāḵôṯ — 'and I commanded that the rooms be cleansed' (ṭāhēr, H2891, piel = to purify, make ritually clean). The cleansing is ceremonial, not merely physical — the rooms had been made ritually impure by foreign occupancy. wāʾāšîḇāh šāmmāh kəlê bêṯ-hāʾĕlōhîm — 'I brought back the vessels of God's house.' The return of the sacred objects to their proper space re-consecrates the room. The paired actions — eject the foreign, restore the sacred — constitute a miniature temple reform, parallel to Hezekiah's cleansing (2 Chronicles 29:5-16) and Jesus's temple clearing (John 2:14-16).
10: wāʾēḏəʿāh kî-mənāyôṯ halwiyyim lōʾ-nittənāh — 'I also learned that the portions (mənāyôṯ, H4521 = appointed shares/portions) of the Levites had not been given.' The verb yāḏaʿ (discovered, came to know) in sequential discovery: temple room first, Levite portions next. wayyibərəḥû ʾîš-ʾel-śāḏēhû — 'each Levite had fled to his field.' The verb bāraḥ (H1272 = to flee, run away; used for refugees fleeing danger) depicts Levites forced to become subsistence farmers because the covenant community's failure to pay tithes made temple service economically impossible.
11: wāʾārîḇāh ʾeṯ-haṣṣəgānîm — 'I brought charges against the officials.' Rîḇ (H7378, qal imperfect) = to contend, plead, bring a lawsuit. This is formal legal accusation, not personal complaint — the same verb as the prophetic rîḇ YHWH ('the LORD brings a lawsuit'), used when God accuses his people of covenant violation (Micah 6:2; Hosea 4:1; Jeremiah 2:9). Nehemiah acts as YHWH's covenant prosecutor against the officials who allowed the Levites' portions to lapse. māddûaʿ neʿĕzaḇ bêṯ-hāʾĕlōhîm — 'Why is the house of God forsaken?' The rhetorical question assumes its own answer: the leaders abandoned their covenant responsibilities.
12: wəḵol-yəhûḏāh hēḇîʾû maʿaśar haddāḡān wəhattîrôš wəhayyiṣhār — 'Then all Judah brought the tithes of grain, new wine, and oil.' The response to leadership accountability: communal covenant compliance. The triple tithe formula (grain, wine, oil) reproduces the Deuteronomic tithe terminology (Deuteronomy 14:23). The qal imperfect hēḇîʾû ('they brought') describes completed, habitual action resumed — the rhythm of tithe-giving restored by Nehemiah's confrontation.
13: wāʾôṣərāh ʿal-hāʾôṣārôṯ — 'I appointed as treasurers (gizbarîm, H1489) over the storehouses.' Four named individuals — a priest, a scribe, a Levite, and an assistant — constitute a transparent, accountable oversight committee. The fourfold structure distributes responsibility across institutional categories (clergy, scribal, Levitical, lay): systemic reform requires systemic accountability structures, not just one trustworthy person. kî nəʾěmānîm neḥšāḇû ʿălêhem — 'for they were considered trustworthy' (nāʾĕman, H539 = faithful, reliable; same root as ʾĕmet). Trustworthiness is the qualification for handling what belongs to God.
14: zāḵərāh-lî ʾĕlōhay ʿal-zōʾt — 'Remember me for this, O my God.' The first of four zāḵar prayers in this chapter (vv14,22,29,31). Zāḵar (H2142 = to remember, act on memory) in Hebrew thought is not passive recollection but active response — divine remembering always produces divine action (Genesis 8:1; Exodus 2:24; 1 Samuel 1:19). The prayer is covenant-based: Nehemiah appeals to YHWH's covenantal memory of faithful service as the ground for divine protection. wəʾal-timmaḥ ḥăsāḏay — 'do not blot out my good deeds.' Māḥāh (H4229 = to wipe out, blot) is the erasure term used for removing names from the book of life (Exodus 32:32-33; Psalms 69:28); ḥeseḏ (H2617) here in the plural means 'deeds of covenant loyalty' — acts done in faithful service.
15: bayyāmîm hāhēmmāh rāʾîṯî bîhûḏāh — 'In those days I saw in Judah.' The first-person eyewitness report grounds the accusation in direct observation, not rumor. Specific Sabbath violations enumerated: dōrəḵîm gittôṯ (treading winepresses), wəmébîʾîm haʿărēmôṯ (bringing in grain-heaps), ʿōmēsîm ʿal-haḥămōrîm (loading donkeys). The verbs are participles (ongoing actions): Nehemiah observes continuous, habitual commerce on the Sabbath. The specificity of the violations (wine, grain, produce) indicates actual agricultural Sabbath-breaking at harvest time.
16: wəhaṣṣōrîm yāšəḇû ḇāh mébîʾîm dāg — 'Tyrians living in the city also brought in fish.' The Phoenician merchants of Tyre introduce commercial pressure from outside the covenant community. Dāg (H1709 = fish) and 'all kinds of merchandise' (kol-māḵār) are sold to bənê yəhûḏāh (the sons of Judah) on the Sabbath. The commercial pressure of Gentile traders makes it impossible for the covenant community to maintain Sabbath distinctiveness without deliberate boundary enforcement — the economic cost of Sabbath observance is real.
17: wāʾārîḇāh ʾeṯ-ḥōrê yəhûḏāh — 'I confronted (rîḇ) the nobles of Judah.' Again the legal-accusation verb: Nehemiah formally charges the nobles with the Sabbath violation. māh-haddāḇār hārāʿ hazzeh ʾăšer ʾattem ʿōśîm — 'What is this evil thing you are doing?' The rhetorical question with hārāʿ (the evil) placed before the participle is emphatic: the evil is named before the action. ûməḥalləlîm ʾeṯ-yôm haššabbāṯ — 'profaning the Sabbath day' (ḥālal, H2490, piel = to defile, profane, pierce). Ḥālal is the defilement verb for violating the sacred — the same root as the forbidden wounding of Aaron's sons (Leviticus 21:9) and Ezekiel's temple-profanation oracles.
18: hălōʾ ḵōh ʿāśû ʾăḇôṯêkem — 'Did not your ancestors do the same?' The appeal to historical precedent: Sabbath violation as a known cause of the Babylonian exile. The rhetorical question invokes corporate memory — the disaster (rāʿāh) that came upon the city is presented as the direct consequence of precisely this behavior. wayyōsîp̄ ʾĕlōhênû ʿālênû ʾeṯ kol-hārāʿāh hazzōʾt — 'our God brought all this disaster upon us.' The historical-theological argument: Sabbath profanation → exile → warning. The prophetic background: Jeremiah's specific Sabbath oracle (Jeremiah 17:21-27) threatened fire on Jerusalem's gates for this exact violation.
19: wayhî kəʾăšer ṣālălû šaʿărê yərûšālaim lip̄nê haššabbāṯ — 'As the gates of Jerusalem were shadowing/darkening before the Sabbath' (ṣālal, H6751 = to cast a shadow, be shaded; the gates begin to cast long afternoon shadows as sunset approaches). The precise sensory image — the shadow of the gates lengthening at dusk — marks the Sabbath onset with visual language. Nehemiah orders the gates shut (ṣiwwîṯî wayyisğərû = I commanded and they were shut). The gate-closing policy is direct economic enforcement: if merchants cannot enter, Sabbath commerce ceases.
20: wayyālînû hārōḵəlîm wəmōḵrê kol-māḵār miḥûṣ lîrûšālaim — 'The merchants and various traders camped outside Jerusalem one or two times.' The traders camp outside the walls hoping to circumvent the gate closure. Their persistence (they come back) demonstrates the economic pressure and tests Nehemiah's resolve. The spatial image — commerce camped just outside the gates — is a vivid picture of the boundary between the sacred city and the commercial world pushing against it.
21: wāʾāʿîḏāh ḇāhem — 'I warned them' (ʿûḏ, H5749, hiphil = to testify against, warn formally). The formal warning is an escalation from confrontation: the first response to Sabbath violations was rebuke (v17); now Nehemiah issues a formal legal warning with consequences. ʾim-tālînû mipaʿam hāʾēd šalaḥtî yāḏ bāḵem — 'If you spend the night again, I will use force against you' (lit. 'I will send my hand against you'). Šālaḥ yāḏ (H7971 + H3027 = send the hand) is a Hebrew idiom for violent enforcement — the threat is physical, not merely legal.
22: wāʾōmar lalwiyyim lihyôṯ miṭṭahărîm ûḇāʾîm šōmərê haššəʾārîm — 'I commanded the Levites to purify themselves and come guard the gates.' Levitical gate-guarding is a priestly act: the gates of the holy city require ritual purity in their guardians. The Levitical function as boundary-keepers between sacred and profane space (Numbers 3:10,38) is activated for the Sabbath enforcement. zāḵərāh-lî ʾĕlōhay ʿal-zōʾt — 'Remember me for this, O my God.' The second memorial prayer (v22; cf. v14). Wəḥûsāh ʿālay — 'and spare me' (ḥûs, H2347 = to have compassion on, spare, look with pity). The addition of the compassion appeal alongside the memorial request shows Nehemiah's awareness that his covenant service does not automatically merit reward — he appeals to grace (ḥûs) as well as covenant faithfulness (zāḵar).
23: gam bayyāmîm hāhēm rāʾîṯî ʾeṯ-hayyəhûḏîm — 'In those days I also saw Jews who had married.' The third major violation: intermarriage with women from Ashdod, Ammon, and Moab. The same three-region list as Ezra 9:1 (with the addition of Ashdod). Rāʾîṯî ('I saw') again grounds the accusation in personal observation. The three communities — Ashdodite (coastal Philistine), Ammonite (east), Moabite (southeast) — represent the full geographic compass of foreign marriage threats surrounding Judah.
24: ûḇənêhem ḥăṣî mədabbēr ʾašdôḏîṯ — 'Half of their children spoke the language of Ashdod.' This is the most distinctive sociolinguistic verse in the Hebrew Bible: the language of the covenant people — yəhûḏîṯ (H3066 = Judean/Hebrew, the covenant language) — is being lost in the second generation through intermarriage. ʾEnam makkirîm ləḏabbēr yəhûḏîṯ — 'they did not know how to speak Judean.' The verb nāḵar (H5234, hiphil = to recognize, know) — they could not recognize or produce the covenant language. The stakes: if the language is lost, the Torah cannot be read (Neh 8), the Psalms cannot be prayed, the covenant vocabulary cannot be preserved.
25: wāʾārîḇ ʿimmāhem wāʾăqallēlem — 'I confronted them and reviled them' (qālaḵ, H7043, piel = to revile, dishonor, curse). wāʾakkeh mēhem ʾănāšîm — 'I struck some of them' (hiphil of nāḵāh, H5221 = to smite, strike; the standard verb for physical violence). wāʾemraṭem — 'I pulled out their hair' (māraṭ, H4803 = to pull out hair, scrape; cf. Ezra 9:3 where Ezra pulls out his own hair in mourning; here the action is coercive, applied to others). The physical confrontation is the most violent action Nehemiah records in his memoir. The combination of cursing, striking, and pulling out hair constitutes maximum public shaming in ANE custom — designed to be so humiliating that the offenders and observers would not repeat the violation.
26: hălōʾ ʿal-ʾēlleh ḥāṭāʾ-šəlōmōh meleḵ yiśrāʾēl — 'Was it not because of women like these that Solomon king of Israel sinned?' The Solomonic precedent is the most powerful available argument: the king uniquely loved by God (ʾăhûḇ ʾĕlōhāyw), who built the temple Nehemiah has spent his career restoring, was destroyed by this exact pattern. Ḥāṭāʾ (H2398, qal = to sin, miss the mark) is unambiguous. wayyaṭəʾû ʾôṯô hannāšîm hannāḵəriyyôṯ — 'the foreign women caused him to sin' — the hiphil of ṭāʾāh (H2398, causative = to lead astray, cause to sin). The historical-theological argument from precedent is devastating: if Solomon with all his wisdom failed, what hope is there without covenant boundary-maintenance?
27: wəlāḵem haniššāmaʿ laʿăśôṯ ʾeṯ kol-hārāʿāh haggəḏôlāh hazzōʾt — 'Are we then to listen to you and do all this great evil?' The rhetorical question expects a resounding 'no.' The phrase hārāʿāh haggəḏôlāh ('this great evil') is the same formula used of Joseph refusing Potiphar's wife (Genesis 39:9 'How can I do this great evil and sin against God?'). The formula marks the action as categorically wrong — not culturally questionable but morally unambiguous. lim'ōl bêʾlōhênû — 'to be unfaithful to our God' — māʿal (H4603 = covenant treachery, trespass), the same word as Ezra 9:2; Nehemiah 10:2. The intermarriage is not just a social issue but a covenant betrayal.
28: ûmibbənê yôyāḏāʿ ben-ʾĔlyāšîḇ hakkōhēn haggāḏôl — 'One of the sons of Joiada son of Eliashib the high priest' — the high priest's grandson has married the daughter of Sanballat the Horonite (Nehemiah's primary opponent, Nehemiah 2:10; Nehemiah 4:1,7; Nehemiah 6:1-14). This is the highest-profile case: the priestly establishment at its apex is directly connected by marriage to the enemy. wāʾabrîḥēhû mēʿālay — 'I chased him away from me' (hiphil of bāraḥ, H1272 = to flee, cause to flee — exile/expulsion). Josephus (Ant. XI.7-8) identifies this individual as Manasseh and preserves the tradition that he subsequently went to Samaria and established a rival priesthood there, planting the roots of the Samaritan religion.
29: zāḵərāh lāhem ʾĕlōhay ʿal gāʾal hakkəhunnāh — 'Remember them against them, O my God, for defiling the priesthood.' The third zāḵar prayer — but this one is imprecatory rather than petitionary. Unlike vv14,22,31 where Nehemiah asks God to 'remember me,' here he asks God to 'remember them' against them. Gāʾal (H1351 = to pollute, defile, make unclean) designates ritual defilement — the priestly genealogical corruption is a sacral category. wəʿal bərît hakkəhunnāh wəhalwiyyim — 'and the covenant of the priesthood and the Levites.' The priestly covenant (Numbers 25:12-13 — the Phinehas covenant of perpetual priesthood; Malachi 2:4-5 — the Levitical covenant) is the specific covenant violated by intermarriage that compromises genealogical purity for priestly office.
30: wəṭiharaṯtîm mikkol-nēkār — 'Thus I cleansed them from everything foreign.' The summary statement uses the piel of ṭāhēr (H2891 = to purify, cleanse) — Nehemiah himself, acting as a lay reformer with prophetic authority, performs the purification that normally requires priestly function. wāʾaʿămîḏāh maʿămādôṯ — 'I assigned duties' (maʿămāḏôṯ, H4612 = appointed stations/posts). The re-establishment of proper Levitical and priestly posts is the positive complement to the negative purification: remove the foreign, restore the legitimate.
31: wəlaqorban hāʿēṣîm bəʿittîm məzummānôṯ wəlabbikkûrîm — 'and arranged for the wood offering at appointed times and for the firstfruits.' Two covenant obligations restored in summary: the wood offering (Nehemiah 10:34 — a community obligation to supply fuel for the altar), and the firstfruits (bikkûrîm, H1061 = the first ripe produce, the offering that consecrates the whole harvest). Both represent the community's ongoing obligation to fund the temple's daily operation. zāḵərāh-lî ʾĕlōhay ləṭôḇāh — 'Remember me, O my God, for good.' The fourth and final zāḵar prayer closes both the chapter and the book. The entire book of Nehemiah ends with an appeal to divine memory: not with a triumphant summary but with a dependent prayer. The great reformer, despite his achievements, stands at the end in the same posture as at the beginning (Nehemiah 1:11) — a servant who can only appeal to God's faithfulness, not to his own merit. Zāḵərāh-lî ləṭôḇāh — 'remember me for good' — the open-endedness of the prayer, asking for future divine action rather than claiming past reward, is the canonical ending that points beyond Nehemiah's reform to a definitive covenant work that only God himself can accomplish.
